mechanism: Implicit statistical learning extracts regularities from multidimensional feature sequences by computing covariance between dimensions; when features covary perfectly, learning binds them into object representations; when covariance is degraded, learning operates on individual features alone. The system assesses correlation structure across feature values and uses this to determine the granularity of the learned unit.
domain: visual perception, implicit learning, feature binding

mechanism: Directed forgetting of list items depends on context encoding; spaced (distributed) presentations strengthen context-item associations through multiple encoding events; context mismatch between study and test impairs retrieval proportionally to context binding strength. Item strength alone does not affect forgetting magnitude; context strength determines susceptibility to forgetting.
domain: episodic memory, directed forgetting, context effects
distribution: outcome: list-strength effect with context-dependent retrieval

mechanism: Numerical magnitude is automatically activated from different symbolic notations (Arabic, Indian digits) through translation to a common abstract representation; the translation occurs before explicit numerical task demands and is indexed by congruency effects in non-numerical tasks. Automaticity is evidenced by interference from irrelevant numerical dimension during physical matching.

Recognition decisions combine familiarity (automatic item-match signal) and recollection via two retrieval routes: context-to-item (using spatial/list context as cue) and item-to-context (using probe to retrieve original context). Serial position effects on both positive and negative probes reflect strength gradients affecting recollection processes. Intrusion errors occur when both routes conflict between item familiarity and context mismatch.
domain: working memory, episodic recognition, source memory
distribution: outcome: serial position U-shaped for positive probes, parallel for intrusion probes

mechanism: Expectations (stereotypes) provided at retrieval construct illusory recollections by incorporating retrieval-context information into the subjective recollective experience; both guessing and false remembering contribute to stereotype-biased responses, but false remembering accounts for stereotype-consistent errors even under explicit don't-know instructions. Gist memories redintegrate fragmentary verbatim traces consistent with schema.
domain: false memory, source monitoring, stereotype effects
distribution: outcome: higher remember responses to stereotype-consistent incorrect attributions

mechanism: Task switching occupies executive attention proportionally to the time spent switching; time-based resource sharing between concurrent processing and maintenance means that any attention-consuming activity (switching or stimulus degradation) impairs memory trace refreshment equally. Recall decreases monotonically with number of switches independent of concurrent memory load; switch cost remains constant across list lengths.
domain: working memory, cognitive control, task switching
distribution: outcome: recall decreases as function of switch count
